title: Offline Local Search for Online Stochastic Bandits
abstract: Abstract Combinatorial multi-armed bandits provide a fundamental online decision-making environment where a decision-maker interacts with an environment across T T time steps, each time selecting an action and learning the cost of that action. The goal is to minimize regret, defined as the loss compared to the optimal fixed action in hindsight under full-information. There has been substantial interest in leveraging what is known about offline algorithm design in this online setting. Offline greedy and linear optimization algorithms (both exact and approximate) have been shown to provide useful guarantees when deployed online. We investigate local search methods, a broad class of algorithms used widely in both theory and practice, which have thus far been under-explored in this context. We focus on problems where offline local search terminates in an approximately optimal solution and give a generic method for converting such an offline algorithm into an online stochastic combinatorial bandit algorithm with O вҖӢ ( log 3 вҒЎ T ) O(^{3}T) (approximate) regret. In contrast, existing offline-to-online frameworks yield regret (and approximate regret) which depend sub-linearly, but polynomially on T T . We demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by applying it to three online stochastic combinatorial optimization problems: scheduling to minimize total completion time, finding a minimum cost base of a matroid and uncertain clustering.

sections.heading: 1 Introduction
sections.body: Online learning is a cornerstone of modern algorithm design. The bandit setting, in which the learner only receives cost feedback, epitomizes the trade-off between exploration and exploitation . Here, an algorithm makes a sequence of decisions x 1 , x 2 , вҖҰ , x T x_{1},x_{2},,x_{T} over a series of T T discrete time periods. In response to each decision x t x_{t} , the environment generates a cost c t вҖӢ ( x t ) c_{t}(x_{t}) (either adversarially or stochastically ), and the algorithmвҖҷs objective is to minimize its cumulative cost across all T T periods. As feedback to improve future decisions, the algorithm observes each cost c t вҖӢ ( x t ) c_{t}(x_{t}) , which is known as bandit feedback . Notably, the algorithm does not observe what the cost would have been if, instead, a different action was chosen at each period, necessitating a trade-off between exploring the action space to find promising solutions and exploiting previously discovered low-cost solutions. Regret measures the difference between the cumulative cost achieved by an algorithm and that of the best fixed solution under full information. A common objective is to construct algorithms with regret that is sub-linear in T T , i.e., o вҖӢ ( T ) o(T) . In the combinatorial bandit setting , the learner must additionally navigate an exponentially large space of actions, rendering some approaches computationally infeasible. There is substantial interest in developing general frameworks for designing online algorithms, especially frameworks that convert offline algorithms (in either a black- or white-box fashion) into effective bandit algorithms; see, for example, Kalai and Vempala , Kakade et al. , DudГӯk et al. , Niazadeh et al. , and Agarwal et al. . Such frameworks allow us to leverage the wealth of knowledge about offline algorithms to construct online learning algorithms. Notable techniques for designing offline algorithms that have proven to be successful in вҖҳoffline-to-onlineвҖҷ frameworks include greedy algorithms and offline linear optimization in both exact and approximate settings. These all utilize sophisticated algorithm design and analysis techniques to convert the offline algorithm to an effective bandit learning algorithm with regret (or Оі -regret ) which scales sub-linearly in T T , more specifically, with regret which scales as T ПҒ T^{} for some ПҒ вҲҲ ( 0 , 1 ) (0,1) (usually ПҒ = 1 / 2 =1/2 or ПҒ = 2 / 3 =2/3 ). The focus of our paper is local search вҖ” another technique widely used in the design of offline algorithms. A local search algorithm consists of three components: a set of feasible solutions рқ’і , a neighborhood map рқ’© : рқ’і вҶ’ 2 рқ’і : 2^{} , and a cost function рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ : рқ’і вҶ’ в„қ }: . Starting from an initial feasible solution x 0 вҲҲ рқ’і x_{0} , local search methods iteratively set x t + 1 = arg вҒЎ min x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x t ) вҒЎ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) x_{t+1}=_{x^{}(x_{t})}}(x^{}) , for t = 1 , 2 , вҖҰ t=1,2, (for a minimization problem), continuing until a locally optimal solution, i.e., a solution x вҲҲ рқ’і x such that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x ) вүӨ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) }(x)}(x^{}) for all x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) , is found. Often, to increase efficiency, a local move is accepted only if it improves upon the current solution by a ОІ -factor, for some ОІ вҲҲ ( 0 , 1 ) (0,1) , and we instead converge to an approximate locally optimal solution. This method is more formally described in Algorithm Лң 1 . By setting ОІ sufficiently close to 1, the resulting local search method can often be shown to converge in a polynomial number of iterations. In practice, local search methods are appealing due to their ease of implementation, efficiency, and ability to find good solutions quickly . Theoretical approximation guarantees have also been established for local search methods for a variety of problems . Despite this widespread use in offline algorithm design, local search has been under-explored as a framework for designing combinatorial bandit algorithms. In fact, as far as we are aware, the connection between local search and bandit algorithms has been in the opposite direction вҖ” several papers use multi-armed bandit algorithms to improve the performance of local search algorithms in practical settings . Thus, the main question we study in this paper is: Can we convert a good offline local search algorithm into an effective online bandit learning algorithm? If so, what regret guarantees can be achieved using this framework? While direct comparisons between offline-to-online frameworks is difficult, this is a substantially better dependence on T T than the O вҖӢ ( T 2 / 3 ) {O}(T^{2/3}) Оі -regret guarantee obtained by Niazadeh et al. via offline greedy algorithms, at the cost of multiplicative factors that depend on the problem and local search neighborhood. The resulting algorithm, in essence, attempts to mimic Algorithm Лң 1 while handling the complications that sprout from the fact that only the stochastically realized cost of the solution submitted in each period is observed. First, the cost of a solution must be estimated by sampling for several time periods. The algorithm attempts to maintain, with high probability, a solution with expected cost below a threshold, which decreases geometrically over time. Whenever the estimated cost of the current solution exceeds this threshold, the solutions in its neighborhood is explored. Neighboring solutions must be carefully sampled to ensure that even neighbors with very high costs do not incur large regret, at the same time, we need enough samples to identify ОІ -improving moves with high probability whenever the current solution is not a Оі -approximation of the optimal solution. This is done by using a successive-elimination style algorithm on the neighborhood. When a ОІ -improving move is found during exploration it becomes the new current solution. This process repeats until time T T or a local optimum is found. To round out our results, we apply our framework to the repeated online bandit feedback versions of three problems: scheduling to minimize total completion time under stochastic job sizes, finding a minimum cost base in a matroid under stochastic element costs, and uncertain k k -median clustering. We show the neighborhood consisting of swapping a single pair of jobs is ( 1 вҲ’ Пө / n 2 , 1 + Пө ) (1-/n^{2},1+) -improving. As a result, we have an algorithm for minimizing online total completion time with ( 1 + Пө ) (1+) -regret in the order of O вҖӢ ( n 12 вҖӢ log 3 вҒЎ T / Пө 4 ) O({n^{12}^{3}T}/{^{4}}) . For the problem of finding a minimum cost base for a matroid, a feasible solution is a base, or maximal independent set, B B . Given stochastic realizations of element costs Z Z , the cost of base B B is рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( B ) = вҲ‘ s вҲҲ B Z s }(B)=_{s B}Z_{s} . We show the neigborhood which consist of bases constructed by adding an arbitrary element to the current solution and removing an element from the resulting circuit is ( 1 вҲ’ Пө / ( 2 вҖӢ r ) , 1 + Пө ) (1-/(2r),1+) -improving, where r r is the rank of the matroid. This implies an online algorithm with ( 1 + Пө ) (1+) -regret in the order of O вҖӢ ( n вҖӢ r 6 вҖӢ log 3 вҒЎ T / Пө 4 ) O({nr^{6}^{3}T}/{^{4}}) , where n n is the size of the ground set. A special case of this result establishes a similar regret bound for the problem of finding minimum cost spanning trees in a graph. In the uncertain k k -median clustering problem the locations of n n points are sampled in a metric space with diameter 1 and the task is to select k k cluster centers from a set of m m potential centers to minimize the cost, defined as the sum of distances from each point to its closest cluster center. As discussed above, we study a combinatorial bandit setting , and our work is closest to other вҖҳoffline-to-onlineвҖҷ frameworks including Kalai and Vempala and DudГӯk et al. , where it is assumed that the offline version of the problem can be easily solved, and Niazadeh et al. , which considers problems with robust greedy approximation algorithms satisfying a property called Blackwell reducibility . We also highlight the recent work of Agarwal et al. which gives a general framework for online learning in monotone stochastic optimization problems achieving O вҖӢ ( T вҖӢ log вҒЎ T ) O() regret against the best approximation algorithm for the offline problem under known distributions. Our results hold for the pure bandit setting where only the cost information is observed. Next, we briefly touch on other related topics. Due to the existence of strong lower bounds in many online learning settings , it is typically necessary to make additional assumptions concerning the environment, or relax the benchmark, in order to achieve regret bounds improving beyond the typical O вҖӢ ( T ПҒ ) O(T^{}) for some ПҒ вҲҲ ( 0 , 1 ) (0,1) . For example, in online convex optimization, logarithmic regret is achievable if the sequence of convex functions satisfies strong convexity . Similarly, logarithmic regret may be possible if certain problem-dependent parameters are bounded appropriately. For example, Xu and Wang achieve logarithmic regret in feature-based dynamic pricing whenever the minimum eigenvalue of a problem-dependent matrix is bounded from below, and Vera et al. achieve logarithmic regret for contextual bandits with knapsacks whenever the weight of an item is bounded from below. In contrast to assuming additional structure, we give poly-logarithmic regret guarantees against a relaxed benchmark, so-called Оі -regret. Our bound is not problem-dependent, and holds for a wide class of instances as long as the local search neighborhood admits ( ОІ , Оі ) (,) -improving moves. For settings where the corresponding offline problem is NP-hard, it is necessary to allow a multiplicative approximation factor. our result is especially interesting in this context, since we achieve Оі -regret that scales as O вҖӢ ( log 3 вҒЎ T ) O(^{3}T) while prior frameworks including those due to Kakade et al. and Niazadeh et al. have Оі -regret which scales as T 2 / 3 T^{2/3} . The exploration strategies specified by many standard approaches to bandit problems can be computationally intractable for large action spaces . As a result, there is significant interest in developing algorithms with more efficient implementations . Since our algorithm is based on local search and performs exploration locally, it naturally lends itself to a computationally efficient implementation. We organize the rest of this paper as follows. Section Лң 2 formally sets up our model and recalls preliminary results we need for our analysis. Then we describe our вҖҳoffline-to-onlineвҖҷ algorithm utilizing local search and provide a high-level overview of its analysis in Section Лң 3 . Following this, Section Лң 4 demonstrates the applicability of our framework on the problems we discussed above. Section Лң 5 concludes the paper and discusses potential directions for future work.

Our main interest is giving algorithms with low Оі -regret, which measures the difference in cumulative expected cost between the algorithm and using a Оі -approximate solution for all T T periods, which we can now define formally. For an algorithm рқ’ң , latent distribution рқ’ҹ , time horizon T T , and Оі вүҘ 1 1 , we define to be the T T -period Оі -regret of algorithm рқ’ң on distribution рқ’ҹ , where the expectation is taken with respect to the independent samples ( Z 1 , Z 2 , вҖҰ , Z T ) вҲј рқ’ҹ T (Z_{1},Z_{2},,Z_{T})^{T} and any internal randomness utilized by рқ’ң . The key property of a neighborhood map that we require to achieve strong Оі -regret bounds is that any solution which has expected cost worse than Оі вҖӢ OPT has a neighboring solution which has multiplicative improvement to its cost. Consider the problem specified by feasible set рқ’і , neighborhood map рқ’© , and expected cost function рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ : рқ’і вҶ’ в„қ + }:_{+} as defined above. sections.heading: 3 Stochastic Combinatorial Bandits from Offline Local Search
sections.body: Our algorithm is formally described in Algorithms Лң 2 and 3 . The algorithm operates in a sequence of phases в„“ = 1 , 2 , вҖҰ =1,2, which are managed by the вҖңforвҖқ loop in Algorithm Лң 2 . Each phase keeps track of a solution x в„“ x_{} and a cost threshold Оё в„“ := Оұ в„“ вҲ’ 1 вҖӢ C max = ОІ ( в„“ вҲ’ 1 ) / 2 вҖӢ C max _{}:=^{-1}C_{}=^{(-1)/2}C_{} . The invariant which, due to stochasticity, we wish to maintain with high probability is that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оё в„“ }(x_{})_{} . Thus, assuming рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оё в„“ }(x_{})_{} , in phase в„“ we wish to either determine that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ = Оё в„“ + 1 }(x_{})_{}=_{+1} , find some x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}(x_{}) with рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүӨ Оұ вҖӢ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ = Оё в„“ + 1 }(x^{})}(x_{})_{}=_{+1} , or determine that no such x вҖІ x^{} exists. In the first case we may set x в„“ + 1 = x в„“ x_{+1}=x_{} to maintain the invariant, while in the second we may set x в„“ + 1 = x вҖІ x_{+1}=x^{} to maintain the invariant. In the last case, we will use the fact that рқ’© satisfies Definition Лң 2.2 and no x вҖІ x^{} was found to conclude that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оі вҖӢ OPT }(x_{}) , and thus we use the current solution x в„“ x_{} for all remaining periods. To achieve this, Algorithm Лң 2 first uses solution x в„“ x_{} for N в„“ N_{} time steps to get a rough estimate of its cost, then compares it to a threshold. The threshold is set so that if рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x_{})^{2}_{} then the estimated cost is highly likely to be smaller than the threshold. As discussed above, we then move to phase в„“ + 1 +1 with x в„“ + 1 = x в„“ x_{+1}=x_{} . If instead, the estimated cost is larger than the threshold, we run the subroutine in Algorithm Лң 3 which explores the neighborhood of x в„“ x_{} . This subroutine returns a solution x рқ–ӯрқ–ҫрқ—җ x_{}} , which is either some x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) or x в„“ x_{} . If x рқ–ӯрқ–ҫрқ—җ x_{}} is some x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) , then we set x в„“ + 1 = x рқ–ӯрқ–ҫрқ—җ x_{+1}=x_{}} and move to phase в„“ + 1 +1 . Critically, we will show that with high probability рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x рқ–ӯрқ–ҫрқ—җ ) вүӨ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ = Оё в„“ + 1 }(x_{}})_{}=_{+1} to maintain the invariant in this case. Finally if x рқ–ӯрқ–ҫрқ—җ = x в„“ x_{}}=x_{} , this indicates that nothing significantly better was found and so we break out of the вҖңforвҖқ loop over phase в„“ , setting x рқ–«рқ–әрқ—Ңрқ—Қ = x в„“ x_{}}=x_{} and using x рқ–«рқ–әрқ—Ңрқ—Қ x_{}} until period T T since, by Definition Лң 2.2 , x рқ–«рқ–әрқ—Ңрқ—Қ x_{}} will satisfy рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x рқ–«рқ–әрқ—Ңрқ—Қ ) вүӨ Оі вҖӢ OPT }(x_{}}) with high probability. As discussed, we want Algorithm Лң 3 to either find a neighboring solution which improves upon x в„“ x_{} by at least an Оұ -factor or indicate that no such improvement exists in рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) (x_{}) , and for this to hold with high probability. It is thus inevitable that a significant number of time steps are spent exploring the neighbourhood of x в„“ x_{} . Doing so without incurring high regret requires some care. A first attempt might involve sampling each neighboring solution N в„“ N_{} times, as we did for x в„“ x_{} in Algorithm Лң 2 , and comparing to an appropriate threshold. This may potentially incur linear regret in later phases when a neighboring solution could be significantly worse than the current solution x в„“ x_{} . To avoid this, we sample neighboring solutions cautiously over a series of вҖңsub-phasesвҖқ of increasing length ( Algorithm Лң 3 , line 4), where we progressively increase the amount of sampling only while the neighbouring solution has the potential to improve over x в„“ x_{} . As a result, poor neighbours are eliminated in early subphases, reminiscent of the successive-elimination algorithm for stochastic multi-armed bandits , and sampled geometrically fewer times than the best solutions (and x в„“ x_{} ). This completes the proof. вҲҺ Let L L be the index of the (random) last phase that is encountered in Algorithm Лң 2 . To bound the overall regret, we will bound the total regret in phases with index в„“ < L <L and also show that the regret in the last phase L L is negligible. Before we can do that, we need to define an appropriate sequence of вҖңbad eventsвҖқ which are exceedingly unlikely, for which will be able to bound the regret when conditioning on their negation, as is standard. For each phase в„“ , let в„і в„“ _{} be the event that Algorithm Лң 2 makes it to phase в„“ and let рқ’һ в„“ _{} be the event that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) > Оё в„“ }(x_{})>_{} . Then we define the bad event in phase в„“ as в„¬ в„“ := в„і в„“ вҲ§ рқ’һ в„“ _{}:=_{}_{} and рқ’ў в„“ = В¬ в„¬ в„“ _{}=_{} . We will show that these events are unlikely. Intuitively, this will follow inductively by assuming that в„¬ в„“ _{} is unlikely, and then we can use the definition of our algorithm to bound Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] [_{+1}_{}] and show that в„¬ в„“ + 1 _{+1} is unlikely via a standard decomposition. The key step involves bounding Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] [_{+1}_{}] . For each в„“ , we have Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] вүӨ T вҲ’ 3 [_{+1}_{}] T^{-3} Proving this lemma requires careful analysis of the TestNeighborhood subroutine ( Algorithm Лң 3 ), which we postpone momentarily. For now, we show Lemma Лң 3.4 implies a bound on Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ ] [_{}] . For each в„“ , we have Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ ] вүӨ в„“ вҖӢ T вҲ’ 3 [_{}] T^{-3} . We proceed by induction on в„“ . For the base case when в„“ = 1 =1 , we have that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x 1 ) вүӨ C max вүӨ Оё 1 }(x_{1}) C_{}_{1} , and so Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ 1 ] = 0 вүӨ T вҲ’ 3 [_{1}]=0 T^{-3} , proving the base case. By the law of total probability we have completing the proof of the inductive case and giving the corollary. вҲҺ We now turn to analyzing Algorithm Лң 3 . Consider a phase в„“ of Algorithm Лң 2 in which Algorithm Лң 3 is called on solution x в„“ x_{} and fix an iteration of the outer вҖҳforвҖҷ loop in Algorithm Лң 3 in which solution x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}(x_{}) is considered. We call each iteration of the inner вҖҳforвҖҷ loop in Algorithm Лң 3 a sub-phase , of which there are at most в„“ for each neighboring solution x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) . Ideally, if рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) > Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})>_{} , then we do not make it past sub-phase в„“ when considering solution x вҖІ x^{} (and actually, we need to be slightly more careful than this). The tools we develop here are needed to bound the regret incurred by using each neighboring solution x вҖІ x^{} across each sub-phase. To start, we introduce the following definitions. For each x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) , we say that x вҖІ x^{} is a bad neighbor of x x if рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүҘ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})_{} . Additionally, let рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x ) вҠҶ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) _{B}(x)(x) be the set of bad neighbors of x x and let в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) вҲҲ [ в„“ ] ^{}(x^{})[] be the sub-phase index such that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вҲҲ [ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) , Оё в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) ] }(x^{})[_{^{}(x^{})},_{^{}(x^{})}] for each x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}_{B}(x) . Let в„¬ в„“ вҖІ ^{}_{} be the event that in phase в„“ some bad neighbor x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}_{B}(x_{}) makes it past sub-phase в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) ^{}(x^{}) when Algorithm Лң 3 is called. Note that if any bad neighbor x вҖІ x^{} makes it past sub-phase в„“ , then Algorithm Лң 3 returns x вҖІ x^{} . This completes the proof. вҲҺ Let L L be the index of the (random) last phase that is encountered in Algorithm Лң 2 . To bound the overall regret, we will bound the total regret in phases with index в„“ < L <L and also show that the regret in the last phase L L is negligible. Before we can do that, we need to define an appropriate sequence of вҖңbad eventsвҖқ which are exceedingly unlikely, for which will be able to bound the regret when conditioning on their negation, as is standard. For each phase в„“ , let в„і в„“ _{} be the event that Algorithm Лң 2 makes it to phase в„“ and let рқ’һ в„“ _{} be the event that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) > Оё в„“ }(x_{})>_{} . Then we define the bad event in phase в„“ as в„¬ в„“ := в„і в„“ вҲ§ рқ’һ в„“ _{}:=_{}_{} and рқ’ў в„“ = В¬ в„¬ в„“ _{}=_{} . We will show that these events are unlikely. Intuitively, this will follow inductively by assuming that в„¬ в„“ _{} is unlikely, and then we can use the definition of our algorithm to bound Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] [_{+1}_{}] and show that в„¬ в„“ + 1 _{+1} is unlikely via a standard decomposition. The key step involves bounding Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] [_{+1}_{}] . For each в„“ , we have Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 вҲЈ рқ’ў в„“ ] вүӨ T вҲ’ 3 [_{+1}_{}] T^{-3} Proving this lemma requires careful analysis of the TestNeighborhood subroutine ( Algorithm Лң 3 ), which we postpone momentarily. For the base case when в„“ = 1 =1 , we have that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x 1 ) вүӨ C max вүӨ Оё 1 }(x_{1}) C_{}_{1} , and so Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ 1 ] = 0 вүӨ T вҲ’ 3 [_{1}]=0 T^{-3} , proving the base case. Inductively, we assume that Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ ] вүӨ в„“ вҖӢ T вҲ’ 3 [_{}] T^{-3} and aim to bound Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ + 1 ] [_{+1}] . By the law of total probability we have completing the proof of the inductive case and giving the corollary. вҲҺ We now turn to analyzing Algorithm Лң 3 . Consider a phase в„“ of Algorithm Лң 2 in which Algorithm Лң 3 is called on solution x в„“ x_{} and fix an iteration of the outer вҖҳforвҖҷ loop in Algorithm Лң 3 in which solution x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}(x_{}) is considered. We call each iteration of the inner вҖҳforвҖҷ loop in Algorithm Лң 3 a sub-phase , of which there are at most в„“ for each neighboring solution x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) . Ideally, if рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) > Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})>_{} , then we do not make it past sub-phase в„“ when considering solution x вҖІ x^{} (and actually, we need to be slightly more careful than this). The tools we develop here are needed to bound the regret incurred by using each neighboring solution x вҖІ x^{} across each sub-phase. To start, we introduce the following definitions. For each x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}(x) , we say that x вҖІ x^{} is a bad neighbor of x x if рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүҘ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})_{} . Additionally, let рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x ) вҠҶ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x ) _{B}(x)(x) be the set of bad neighbors of x x and let в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) вҲҲ [ в„“ ] ^{}(x^{})[] be the sub-phase index such that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вҲҲ [ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) , Оё в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) ] }(x^{})[_{^{}(x^{})},_{^{}(x^{})}] for each x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x ) x^{}_{B}(x) . Let в„¬ в„“ вҖІ ^{}_{} be the event that in phase в„“ some bad neighbor x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© B вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}_{B}(x_{}) makes it past sub-phase в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) ^{}(x^{}) when Algorithm Лң 3 is called. Note that if any bad neighbor x вҖІ x^{} makes it past sub-phase в„“ , then Algorithm Лң 3 returns x вҖІ x^{} . Further, if any bad neighbor x вҖІ x^{} makes it past sub-phase в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) ^{}(x^{}) , then we may incur too much regret from testing x вҖІ x^{} in later sub-phases since рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүҘ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) }(x^{})_{^{}(x^{})} . We show that в„¬ в„“ вҖІ ^{}_{} is unlikely, and conclude that bad neighbors are highly likely to be removed from contention early enough to avoid incurring high regret. Pr вҒЎ [ в„¬ в„“ вҖІ ] вүӨ T вҲ’ 4 [^{}_{}] T^{-4} . In the case that Algorithm Лң 3 is not called in phase в„“ , then the probability is at most 0 since в„¬ в„“ вҖІ ^{}_{} cannot happen. To this end, fix a bad neighbor x вҖІ x^{} . If x вҖІ x^{} doesnвҖҷt make it to sub-phase в„“ вҖІ вҖӢ ( x вҖІ ) вүӨ в„“ ^{}(x^{}) , then it clearly doesnвҖҷt make it past sub-phase в„“ . By part(b) of Lemma Лң 3.2 , the probability of this is at most M вҲ’ 1 вҖӢ T вҲ’ 4 M^{-1}T^{-4} . It follows that Finally, observe that which completes the proof. вҲҺ Lemma Лң 3.8 establishes that Algorithm Лң 3 filters out bad neighboring solutions with high probability. In order to prove Lemma Лң 3.4 , we also need to show that good neighboring solutions are unlikely to be filtered out. More specifically, there are three situations to consider (all under the condition that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оё в„“ }(x_{})_{} ): (1) every solution in рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) (x_{}) is a bad neighbor, (2) some solution x вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( x в„“ ) x^{}(x_{}) satisfies рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүӨ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})^{2}_{} , and (3) not all neighboring solutions are bad, but all solutions that are not bad have рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вҲҲ [ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ , Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ ] }(x^{})[^{2}_{},_{}] . In situation (1), it is a simple corollary of Lemma Лң 3.8 that Algorithm Лң 3 will output x в„“ x_{} with high probability, indicating that we did not find an improved solution (and therefore рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оі вҖӢ OPT }(x_{}) ). In situation (2), where there is a neighboring solution x вҖІ x^{} with рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүӨ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ }(x^{})^{2}_{} , it is possible that Algorithm Лң 3 considers and returns a different neighboring solution x вҖІвҖІ x^{} with рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІвҖІ ) вҲҲ [ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ , Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ ] }(x^{})[^{2}_{},_{}] (again it is unlikely to return a bad neighbor due to Lemma Лң 3.8 ). However, if x вҖІ x^{} is ever considered it will survive past sub-phase в„“ with high probability ( Lemmas Лң 3.2 and 3.8 ) and thus be returned by Algorithm Лң 3 . Either outcome is acceptable and guarantees at least an Оұ -factor improvement. In situation (3), Algorithm Лң 3 may output x в„“ x_{} , indicating no improved solution. This follows since, under the event that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оё в„“ }(x_{})_{} , each neighboring solution has рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x вҖІ ) вүҘ Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ вүҘ ОІ вҖӢ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) }(x^{})_{}}(x_{}) , which by the ( ОІ , Оі ) (,) -improving moves condition implies that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( x в„“ ) вүӨ Оі вҖӢ OPT }(x_{}) . It is also acceptable for Algorithm Лң 3 to output a solution with cost in the interval [ Оұ 2 вҖӢ Оё в„“ , Оұ вҖӢ Оё в„“ ] [^{2}_{},_{}] , since that guarantees an Оұ -factor improvement. sections.heading: 4 Applications of Our Framework
sections.body: We consider the problem of scheduling stochastic jobs to minimize total completion time on a single machine. Let рқ’ҹ be a distribution over job sizes which is unknown to the scheduler. There are n n jobs with stochastic sizes P = ( P 1 , P 2 , вҖҰ , P n ) вҲј рқ’ҹ P=(P_{1},P_{2},,P_{n}) . We only require independence across time periods; a pair of jobs j j and j вҖІ j^{} may have correlated size distributions. We also make a standard assumption that the job size distribution is bounded and normalized to be in . After reordering the summation, the cost of a schedule ПҖ is Let OPT := min ПҖ вҒЎ рқ”ј P вҲј рқ’ҹ вҖӢ [ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( ПҖ , P ) ] :=_{}_{P}[}(,P)] . We focus on learning from limited feedback using fixed, non-preemptive schedules. In the single machine case full-information case, it is well known that the optimal schedule is given by applying SmithвҖҷs rule to the expected job sizes: ПҖ вҲ— ^{*} orders the jobs in non-decreasing order of Ој j _{j} , or shortest expected processing time first . When learning to schedule with bandit feedback, the scheduler gains information by interacting with unknown рқ’ҹ through the following process which occurs in each of T T discrete time periods. In period t вҲҲ [ T ] t[T] , the scheduler commits to a schedule ПҖ t ^{t} before job sizes are realized according to an independent sample P t вҲј рқ’ҹ P^{t} . Then the scheduler observes рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( ПҖ t , P t ) }(^{t},P^{t}) , the realized total completion time of schedule ПҖ t ^{t} , but does not observe the realized job sizes P t P^{t} . For a schedule ПҖ , define error вҒЎ ( ПҖ ) := рқ”ј вҖӢ [ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( ПҖ , P ) ] вҲ’ OPT ():=[}(,P)]- to measure the expected regret we incur by using ПҖ for one step. Recall that in this case an optimal schedule ПҖ вҲ— ^{*} must schedule j j after j вҖІ j^{} if and only if Ој j > Ој j вҖІ _{j}>_{j^{}} . sections.heading: 4.3 Uncertain k k -Median Clustering
sections.body: Our final application concerns k k -median clustering where the set of points to be clustered are drawn at random from an unknown distribution. The following setup was studied by Cormode and McGregor and Guha and Munagala for a several of k k -clustering objectives in the offline setting. An instance consists of n n points arriving from a metric space ( в„і , d ) (,d) of diameter 1. The location of the i i вҖҷth point is uncertain and the objective is to choose k k cluster centers which minimize the total expected distance between each arriving point and its closest cluster center. We let Z i вҲҲ в„і Z_{i} denote the random realization of the i i вҖҷth point. For a fixed location z вҲҲ в„і z and a fixed set C C of cluster centers, let d вҖӢ ( z , C ) = min c вҲҲ C вҒЎ d вҖӢ ( z , c ) d(z,C)=_{c C}d(z,c) denote the distance of z z to its nearest cluster center in C C . Then the cost of a solution C C on points Z = ( Z 1 , Z 2 , вҖҰ , Z n ) Z=(Z_{1},Z_{2},,Z_{n}) is Thus, the expected cost of solution C C is given by and OPT denotes the expected cost of a set of cluster centers which minimizes this expected cost. In the bandit setting, we do not observe the realizations of the point locations. In each round t t a set of cluster center locations C t C_{t} is submitted, after which the learner only observes рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( C t , Z ) }(C_{t},Z) . Let рқ’© вҖӢ ( C ) = { C вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’і вҲЈ C вҲӘ { b } вҲ– { a } , a вҲҲ C , b вҲҲ рқ’і вҲ– C } (C)=\{C^{} C\{b\}\{a\},a C,b C\} be the swap-neighborhood of cluster centers C C . Arya et. al. propose a local search algorithm for the k k -medians problem which starts from an arbitrary set of k k cluster centers C C and repeatedly moves to neighboring C вҖІ вҲҲ рқ’© вҖӢ ( C ) C^{}(C) satisfying рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( C вҖІ ) вүӨ ( 1 вҲ’ Пө ) вҖӢ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( C ) }(C^{})(1-)}(C) until no local improvement is found. Cormode and McGregor shows that this is equally effective for the uncertain k k -medians problem. For the uncertain k k -median clustering problem, local search with Пө -improvements terminates in C вҲҲ рқ’і C satisfying рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( C ) вүӨ ( 5 / ( 1 вҲ’ n вҖӢ Пө ) ) вҖӢ рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ңрқ—Қ вҒЎ ( C вҲ— ) . }(C)(5/(1-n))}(C^{*}). The Minimum Cost K K -Median problem with n n input points admits ( ОІ , Оі ) (,) -improving moves where Оі = 5 / ( 1 вҲ’ 1 n ) =5/(1-{n}) and ОІ = ( 1 вҲ’ 1 / n 2 ) =(1-{1}/{n^{2}}) . sections.heading: 5 Discussion
sections.body: We conclude by discussing a couple of relevant issues not raised elsewhere. For ease of exposition we assumed the algorithm knows T T , and that there is an upper bound C max C_{} on costs. It is straightforward but tedious to modify the algorithms and analysis to not require explicit knowledge of T T . Similarly, we can replace the assumption of bounded costs with suitable distributional assumptions about the cost. For example, we could require a sub-gaussian like condition on the tails of the distribution or even just finite variance. In the latter case, we could make use of tools for handling heavy-tailed distributions in the bandit setting . It remains to computationally demonstrate the benefits of our algorithm. All three applications are instances of linear bandits , so a natural starting point may be to compare the regret of our algorithm with that of a linear bandit style algorithm which, in each step, uses the bandit feedback to estimate problem parameters (eg. mean job sizes for completion time scheduling), then computes the solution to an offline instance of the problem with those parameters to submit in the next time step. For the scheduling and matroid applications the offline instances can be solved efficiently, for clustering we have to resort to an approximate solution due to NP-hardness. An exciting direction for future work is exploring further applications for our framework where local search is effective offline. Some applications may require generalizing our current approach, for example, the local search guarantees for set cover rely on finding neighboring solution which improves a specialized potential function, and not the objective function directly. It is an open question whether a version of ( ОІ , Оі ) (,) -improving moves defined on such potential functions can provide guarantees in the bandit online setting.

summary:
Here's a summary of the research paper in clear bullet points:

**Introduction**

* Combinatorial multi-armed bandits is a fundamental online decision-making environment where a decision-maker interacts with an environment across T time steps.
* The goal is to minimize regret, defined as the loss compared to the optimal fixed action in hindsight under full-information.
* Offline algorithms have been shown to provide useful guarantees when deployed online.

**Background**

* Online learning is a cornerstone of modern algorithm design.
* The bandit setting, where the learner only receives cost feedback, epitomizes the trade-off between exploration and exploitation.
* Regret measures the difference between the cumulative cost achieved by an algorithm and that of the best fixed solution under full information.

**Local Search Methods**

* Local search methods are a broad class of algorithms used widely in both theory and practice.
* They have been under-explored in the context of online stochastic combinatorial bandit algorithms.
* Offline local search terminates in an approximately optimal solution.

**Converting Offline Local Search to Online Algorithm**

* We propose a generic method for converting offline local search into an online stochastic combinatorial bandit algorithm.
* The resulting algorithm has O(log^3 T) (approximate) regret.
* This is in contrast to existing offline-to-online frameworks, which yield regret (and approximate regret) that depend sub-linearly, but polynomially on T.

**Applications**

* We demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by applying it to three online stochastic combinatorial optimization problems:
	+ Scheduling to minimize total completion time
	+ Finding a minimum cost base of a matroid
	+ Uncertain clustering

**Conclusion**

* Our framework provides a new approach to converting offline local search into online stochastic combinatorial bandit algorithms.
* The resulting algorithms have improved regret bounds compared to existing offline-to-online frameworks.
* Our framework has the potential to be applied to a wide range of online stochastic combinatorial optimization problems.
